title: Citron, a Rho target that affects contractility during cytokinesis
abstract:
Citron, a Rho Target That Affects Contractility During
Cytokinesis
PASCAL MADAULE, TOMOYUKI FURUYASHIKI, MASATOSHI EDA, HARUHIKO BITO,
TOSHIMASA ISHIZAKI, AND SHUH NARUMIYA*
Department of Pharmacology, Kyoto University Faculty of Medicine, Sakyo-ku, Kyoto 606-8315, Japan
KEY WORDS:
Rho; cytokineses; midbody; actin; kinase; CNH; ROCK; contraction
ABSTRACT
The small GTPase Rho, which regulates cell shape, is thought to contribute to
cytokinesis. Recently, Citron was characterized as a Rho target. This large protein contains a
Ser/Thr kinase domain related to that of ROCK, another Rho effector. Both endogenous Citron and
recombinant Citron localize to the cleavage furrow in dividing cells and to the midbody in post-
mitotic cells. Moreover, overexpression of Citron deleted from its C-terminal sequence caused
abnormal contractions speciﬁcally during cytokinesis, resulting in the formation of multinucleated
cells. Cell shape, F-actin, intermediate ﬁlaments, and microtubules appeared essentially normal in
these cells during interphase. Thus, Citron is a Rho effector that appears to function during
cytokinesis, modulating its contractile process. In brain, however, Citron is highly expressed in a
subset of neurons as a brain-speciﬁc isoform that lacks a kinase domain, Citron-N. This protein
accumulates in synapses and associates to the NMDA receptor via interaction with the adaptor
protein PSD95, suggesting that the function of Citron is specialized in the neurons. Microsc. Res.
Tech. 49:123–126, 2000.
© 2000 Wiley-Liss, Inc.
INTRODUCTION
The small GTPases form a large and diverse family
of proteins typiﬁed by the proto-oncogene Ras. They
function as molecular switches in signal transduction
pathways. Upon proper stimulation, they bind GTP
and may stimulate various target proteins, or effectors,
causing an appropriate response of the cell. Upon GTP
hydrolysis, the small GTPases return to the GDP-
bound resting state.
RhoA, RhoB, and RhoC are three highly related
small GTPases that are involved in regulation of cell
shape including myosin-based contraction, cell adhe-
sion, cell-cell interactions, cell migration, and neurite
development (for review see Narumiya, 1996). The
Rho-related proteins Rac and Cdc42 are also involved
in similar regulations and all these proteins markedly
affect the actin cytoskeleton. The Rho family also reg-
ulates cytokinesis because microinjection of either re-
combinant mutant Rho or Cdc42 proteins into a divid-
ing egg causes a failure of cytokinesis. Conversely,
microinjection of exoenzyme C3, a bacterial toxin that
inactivates Rho, also caused cytokinesis defect in divid-
ing eggs (Drechsel et al., 1997; Kishi et al., 1993; Ma-
buchi et al., 1993). The molecular mechanisms by
which the Rho family regulates cytokinesis, however,
were unknown.
In this article, we will summarize our current knowl-
edge about Citron, a recently discovered Rho target
that appeared to function in cytokinesis.
ISOLATION AND STRUCTURE OF CITRON,
A RHO TARGET
Citron was ﬁrst isolated using a yeast two-hybrid
system. With RhoC as a bait, we screened a mouse
embryo cDNA library and obtained about 30 clones
displaying interaction with the RhoC bait. A single
clone, named Citron, had a pattern of reactivity ex-
pected for a Rho target: stronger interaction with a
dominant active Rho-bait (constitutively bound to
GTP) and barely detectable interaction with a domi-
nant negative Rho-bait (constitutively bound to GDP).
The interaction with Citron was conﬁrmed directly,
using recombinant proteins according to a method
known as the overlay assay. Data indicated that Citron
interacts with the active form of RhoA and RhoC, but
not with that of Cdc42 (Madaule et al., 1995). Regard-
ing Rac, an interaction could be detected in the overlay
assay but could not be conﬁrmed in the two-hybrid
system (Fujisawa et al., 1998).
Citron gene, which has been located near the
MODY3 locus on human chromosome 12 (Yamagata et
al., 1996), encodes for multiple splicing isoforms, the
longest being Citron-K with a predicted size of 230
kDa. This protein may be divided into three regions
(Fig. 1): an N-terminal Ser/Thr kinase domain, a cen-
tral domain predicted to form a coiled-coil, and a C-
terminal region with several motifs involved in signal
transduction, namely, the Rho-binding domain, a zinc
ﬁnger, a PH domain, a domain recently discovered
(Schultz et al., 1998) and named CNH (CitroN Homol-
ogy), a putative SH3 partner domain, and a PDZ part-
ner domain (Madaule et al., 1995, 1998; Furuyashiki et
al., 1999). This overall domain structure (kinase,
coiled-coil, signal transduction motifs) is reminiscent of
that of ROCK-I and II (also known as ROKb and ROKa,
Pascal Maduale’s present address is Re´cepteurs et signalisation des interleu-
kines, INSERM U 461, Faculte´ de Pharmacie de l’Universite´ d’Orsay, 5 rue Jean
Baptiste Cle´ment, 92296 Chaˆtenay-Malabry, France.
*Correspondence to Shuh Narumiya, Department of Pharmacology, Kyoto
University Faculty of Medicine, Sakyo-ku, Kyoto 606-8315, Japan.
Received 7 September 1999; accepted 15 October 1999
MICROSCOPY RESEARCH AND TECHNIQUE 49:123–126 (2000)
© 2000 WILEY-LISS, INC.
 respectively), which are two isoforms of a Rho target
(Ishizaki et al., 1996; Leung et al., 1996), and to that of
Genghis Khan/MRCK, a Cdc42 effector (Leung et al.,
1998; Luo et al., 1997). The catalytic kinase domains of
these proteins are roughly 50% identical to that of
Citron in pairwise comparisons, while the other regions
are more diverse.
Citron-K is ubiquitously expressed in tissue culture
cell lines of various origin (Fig. 2) but in adult organs
the expression is variable (Madaule et al. 1995; Di
Cunto at al., 1998); highest expression was observed in
testis and to a weaker extent in spleen and thymus.
Beside Citron-K, we isolated a splice variant that lacks
the kinase domain and was named Citron-N because
its expression seems restricted to the brain. A 126-bp-
long exon from the coiled-coil also is subject to alterna-
tive splicing (Madaule et al., 1995,1998). Recently, Di
Cunto et al. (1998) described a splice variant that con-
tains just the kinase domain (Fig. 1). These authors
also reported that binding of Rho-GTP to Citron-K
moderately increases its in vitro kinase activity, a
property shared by ROCK.
CITRON-K AFFECTS THE CONTRACTILE
PROCESS OF CYTOKINESIS
Immunoﬂuorescence analysis of HeLa cells, using
antibodies directed against Citron, detected a signal
concentrated at the cleavage furrow of dividing cells
(Fig. 3A). A similar pattern was observed when the
cells were transiently transfected with Myc-tagged
Citron and stained with antibodies toward Myc (Fig.
3B). To gain further insight into the function of Citron,
several C-terminal truncation mutants as well as ki-
nase inactive alleles (Lys126, from the ATP binding
site, mutated to Ala) were prepared and introduced
into HeLa cells. Three days after transfection, a signif-
icant proportion of the transfected cells had become
multinucleated, including cells expressing an allele de-
void of both kinase activity and Rho-binding site. In the
case of D1-Citron-K, in which the sequences down-
stream of the Rho-binding site have been removed,
nearly all of the transfected cells were multinucleated
3 days after transfection. Because the phenotype of this
mutant is very strong, we could follow by video micro-
scopy how these cells fail to complete cytokinesis. HeLa
cells were cotransfected with D1-Citron-K and a plas-
mid-encoding green ﬂuorescent protein. Sixteen to 20
hours after transfection, transfected cells were identi-
ﬁed by their green ﬂuorescence and metaphasic cells
were examined by time lapse video microscopy using
phase-contrast transmitted light. At this early time
point, cells were performing their ﬁrst attempt to di-
vide in the presence of mutant Citron. Typically, the
chromosomes separate and the actin ring initiates con-
traction. The transfected cells, however, consistently
fail to establish the intercellular bridge and regress
furrowing within 1 hour. In about 20% of these cells,
the cleavage furrow ceases contracting prematurely
and cytokinesis fails immediately. The most remark-
able aspect of the phenotype is its abnormal contractile
movement that manifests in about half of the recorded
cells. In these instances, one of the prospective daugh-
ter cells contracts inequally, transferring most of its
content to its partner, which then contracts and pushes
back the transferred material as well as its own. These
push-pull
movements
are
rapid,
approximately
1
minute per cycle, and occur repeatedly. It is worth
noting that these abnormal contractile movements re-
quire the kinase activity because they were not ob-
served in cells expressing the kinase inactive allele of
D1-Citron-K. Thus, we concluded that Citron-K is a
Rho effector that participates in cytokinesis and affects
its contractile process (Madaule et al., 1998).
CITRON-K ACCUMULATES IN THE MIDBODY
After cytokinesis, animal cells remain connected for
at least a few hours by a tubulin-rich structure, the
intercellular bridge. The middle of this bridge contains
a dense nodule known as the midbody (Byers and
Abramson, 1968). The function of this organelle is not
Fig. 1.
Schematic presentation of Citron
and related proteins. Alternative splicing
generates the three Citron proteins. ROCK-I
is another Rho effector and MRCKa is an
effector of Cdc42, a Rho-related small GT-
Pase. The percentages of identities to Citron
are shown for the kinase domain and the
coiled-coil domain. PH: plextrin homology do-
main; CNH: Citron homology domain.
Fig. 2.
Northern blot analysis of Citron in various cell lines.
Twenty milligrams of polyA1 RNA were analyzed for each cell line.
The Citron probe was the full-length encoding sequence of Citron-N,
labeled with
32P. The positions of the ribosomal RNA are indicated.
124
P. MADAULE ET AL.

concentrated at the cleavage furrow of dividing cells

Citron and stained with antibodies toward Myc (Fig.

ter cells contracts inequally, transferring most of its

served in cells expressing the kinase inactive allele of

Rho effector that participates in cytokinesis and affects
its contractile process (Madaule et al., 1998).

at least a few hours by a tubulin-rich structure, the
intercellular bridge. The middle of this bridge contains
a dense nodule known as the midbody (Byers and
Abramson, 1968). The function of this organelle is not

known, though it allows transfer of macromolecules
between the daughter cells (Schulze and Blose, 1984).
Citron-K was detected as a bright signal in the mid-
body (Fig. 3C). Further analysis indicated that a siz-
able amount of Rho also concentrates in the midbody,
detectable with either monoclonal or polyclonal anti-
bodies to RhoA (Fig. 3D and E). The detection of Rho,
however, was restricted to the periphery of the mid-
body. The absence of Rho signal in the center of the
midbody is difﬁcult to interpret because immunoﬂuo-
rescence analysis of this region is often negative for
proteins known to be present, for example, tubulin.
Although the localization of Rho and Citron-K in the
midbody might represent remnant structures required
for cytokinesis, it is tempting to speculate that these
proteins serve a speciﬁc function in the intercellular
bridge (Madaule et al., 1998).
COMPARISON OF CITRON-K WITH ROCK
ROCK, a Rho effector related to Citron, has been
extensively analyzed. Cells overexpressing this en-
zyme, or its more active C-terminal truncation mu-
tants, display a retracted phenotype and accumulate
actin stress ﬁbers (Ishizaki et al., 1997; Leung et al.,
1996). Biochemically, it has been reported that ROCK
down-regulates myosin light chain phosphatase, caus-
ing the actomyosin system to contract (Kimura et al.,
1996). Recently, ROCK was also shown to up-regulate
LIM-kinase, which phosphorylates coﬁlin, causing a
stabilization of F-actin (Maekawa et al., 1999). Thus,
ROCK seemed to be a good candidate as a mediator of
Rho during cytokinesis. Indeed, others recently ob-
served that ROCK accumulates into the cleavage fur-
row of dividing cells (Kosako et al., 1999). However, in
our hands, cells overexpressing ROCK or its C-termi-
nal deletion mutants do not become multinucleate, nor
do the cells treated with Y-27632, a pyrimidine deriv-
ative that speciﬁcally inhibits the kinase activity of
ROCK. In contrast, cells overexpressing Citron-K or its
mutants do not form many stress ﬁbers nor do they
display a retracted phenotype; instead, abnormal con-
tractions are observed during cytokinesis and these
cells become multinucleate (Madaule et al., 1998).
Thus, we propose that myosin-based contractility
would be regulated by ROCK during interphase and by
Citron-K during cytokinesis. This model does not rule
out a function for ROCK during cell division, but sug-
gests that ROCK may be dispensable in the presence of
COUPLING RHO TO THE NMDA RECEPTOR
Citron-N, a splice variant that lacks the kinase domain
and seems to be speciﬁc to the brain (Furuyashiki et
al., 1999; Zhang et al., 1999). Western blots and in situ
hybridizations performed on adult mouse tissues indi-
cated that Citron-N is abundant in the thalamus and is
also easily detected in the cortex and the cerebellum.
Most of the expression occurs in a subset of neurons,
however. In a development analysis of the cerebellum,
Citron-K expression paralleled the mitotic index of this
organ, while expression of Citron-N paralleled that of
Immunoﬂuorescence analysis of HeLa cells. A: A dividing
cell stained with a rabbit antiserum against Citron (1:300). B: A
dividing cell transfected with Myc-tagged Citron-K deleted down-
stream of the Rho-binding site. This picture represents a single con-
focal section. Cells were stained with 9E10, a monoclonal antibody to
Myc. C: Cells stained for tubulin with a monoclonal antibody (red) and
for Citron with a rabbit antiserum (green; 1:300). D: Cells stained
with a commercial polyclonal antibody to RhoA (Santa Cruz Biotech-
nology Inc., Santa Cruz, CA; 1:100). E: Cells stained with a commer-
cial monoclonal antibody to RhoA (Santa Cruz Biotechnology Inc.;
1:100). The large dots detected in the cell interior by the monoclonal
antibody to RhoA might represent a spurious crossreactivity. The
arrows in D and E point at the midbodies. No signal was observed
when the ﬂuorescent secondary antibodies were used in the absence of
primary antibodies. The cells were not transfected, except in B.
CITRON AS A RHO EFFECTOR FOR CYTOKINESIS
proteins constitutive of the synapses, PSD-95 and
CaMKII. Furthermore, Citron-N and PSD-95 could be
coimmunopreciptated from brain lysates. PSD-95 is an
adaptor protein that bears three PDZ domains and
associates to the NMDA receptor in the postsynaptic
density. In the two-hybrid system, PSD-95 interacted
with the C-terminus of Citron, which indeed bears a
PDZ partner consensus sequence. Finally, we could
coimmunoprecipitate the ternary complex composed of
NMDA receptor subunits, PSD-95 and Citron-N, from
CHO-cotransfected cells. Thus, the Rho pathway is
likely to be physically linked to the NMDA receptor by
PSD-95, at least in a subset of neurons. Since the
NMDA receptor has been implicated in learning and
synaptic plasticity, and since Rho protein regulates
neurite development and cytoskeleton, it would be in-
teresting to see how the Rho pathway is utilized for
regulating synaptic plasticity, downstream of the
